text: Two Allis forceps are applied on either side of the umbilicus to alleviate the crease, preparing the area for laparoscopic entry.

A 2mm stab wound is created in the abdominal wall, which measures 2 centimeters in thickness. An 8cm Veress needle is used, with only 3 to 4 centimeters of the needle tip exposed, while the rest is guarded by the surgeon's finger.

The surgeon carefully inserts the varus needle into the abdominal wall, ensuring it enters at a ninety-degree angle to the wall and a forty-five-degree angle to the patient's body. Two audible clicks are heard, indicating proper placement, followed by irrigation and suction tests to confirm needle positioning.

The surgeon performs the hanging drop test by placing a few drops of saline on the varus needle and lifting it, observing the saline being sucked in. Subsequently, the insufflator is attached carefully for pediatric surgery, ensuring the gas flow is initially set obliquely within 100 ml to avoid over-insufflation.
The surgeon observes the achieved abdominal distension, indicating successful insufflation. The Veress needle is carefully held and then withdrawn from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon enlarges the incision over the inferior crease of the umbilicus, creating a 'smiling' shape to facilitate access for the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses mosquito forceps to dilate the inferior crease of the umbilicus, targeting the obliterated vitreous intestinal duct as part of a modified Scandinavian technique.

The surgeon inserts a 10-millimeter trocar, ensuring only 5 centimeters of the trocar is exposed. Using a screwing motion, the trocar is advanced into the abdominal cavity. The trocar is then removed, leaving the cannula in place for further use.

The surgeon introduces the insufflator and carefully inserts the telescope into the abdominal cavity, considering the limited space and thinner abdominal wall in pediatric patients. Transillumination is performed using a Striker mini alligator instrument.

The surgeon uses a 1.8 mm Striker mini alligator grasper, which is percutaneously inserted without a cannula, to hold the appendix. This instrument stabilizes the abdominal wall and leaves no scar. A harmonic scalpel is introduced through another port, indicating a three-port appendectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a Striker mini alligator grasper to hold the appendix securely, locking it in place to prevent movement. The surgical setup includes ports in the right hypochondrium, right iliac fossa, and left iliac fossa, facilitating access for the appendectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic instrument in the left iliac fossa to hold the appendix and retract it away, creating more space for the appendectomy procedure. This technique utilizes mini alligators for a two-port approach, suitable for both pediatric and adult patients.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device, positioning its silicon jaw towards the appendix to prevent heat transfer during dissection. The major appendix is taken, and the appendicular artery is dissected.
The surgeon exposes the cecum, preparing to skeletonize it to access the base of the appendix, ensuring a complete appendectomy.
The surgeon has reached the base of the appendix and performs peritoneal mobilization to fully expose the base for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the harmonic scalpel near the appendix, ensuring that the tip does not accidentally contact the ileum or cecum.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the major appendix and prepares a Misra's knot outside the abdomen using a number one vial and a darker knot pusher. The knot is then introduced into the abdomen, where the striker mini alligator is used to catch the tip of the appendix and bring it through the loop.

The surgeon uses a knot pusher to position the Misra's knot over the base of the appendix, ensuring the knot slips securely at the junction with the cecum to prevent leaving any stump. The knot is tied tightly around the base, suitable for structures up to eighteen millimeters in diameter.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate and cut the remaining stump of the appendix, ensuring the mucosa is cauterized to prevent spillage of fecal matter. The harmonic scalpel holds the stump while cutting, and care is taken to avoid contact with the cecum to prevent perforation.
The surgeon uses the rail rod technique to extract the appendix through a ten millimeter cannula, holding the tip of the appendix with a grasper and guiding it into the cannula.
A grasper holds the tip of the appendix, and the surgeon carefully maneuvers it towards the laparoscopic telescope for better visualization.

The surgeon uses a grasper to push the appendix into the cannula of the telescope. Under direct vision, the telescope observes the stump of the appendix as it is gradually pulled out. The telescope is withdrawn while the cannula is negotiated, ensuring the appendix is extracted without damaging surrounding structures. The zero-degree view provides a clear image, while the thirty-degree view shows only a partial view of the stump.
